my_sentence: A kubernetes cluster is like a group of machine. If someone have problems, it will affect your application.
correction: A kubernetes cluster is like a group of machines. If one of them has a problem, it can affect your application.
explanation: After 'a group of' the noun must be plural ('machines'). Also, a singular subject ('one' / 'someone') takes the -s form of the verb: 'has', not 'have'.
correct_examples: A cluster is a group of machines working together.
If one node has a problem, the others keep running.
context: Explaining what a Kubernetes cluster is

my_sentence: If some of them had some problem, they not will affect the application.
correction: If some of them have a problem, they will not affect the application.
explanation: In a negative statement the word 'not' goes after the auxiliary/modal verb: 'will not', not 'not will'.
correct_examples: They will not affect the service.
It will not change the result.
context: Explaining redundancy across machines in a cluster

my_sentence: Every day there are many customers go to these stores.
correction: Every day there are many customers who go to the stores.
explanation: You cannot put two finite verbs ('are' and 'go') side by side with the same subject. Use a relative clause ('customers who go') to connect them.
correct_examples: There are many people who visit the store every day.
We have customers who shop here daily.
context: Describing how many customers the company's stores serve

my_sentence: When I come back, I didn't find anyone in the home.
correction: When I came back, I didn't find anyone at home.
explanation: The two clauses describe the same past event, so 'come' must be in the past tense ('came') to agree with 'didn't'.
correct_examples: When I came back, the house was empty.
When I got home, nobody was there.
context: Practising adverbial clauses with 'when' to express a complaint

my_sentence: I didn't find anyone in the home.
correction: I didn't find anyone at home.
explanation: The fixed expression is 'at home' (meaning at one's house), not 'in the home'. 'The home' sounds institutional and is not used here.
correct_examples: Nobody was at home.
I stayed at home all weekend.
context: Practising adverbial clauses with 'when' to express a complaint

my_sentence: Mr. John said he wanted to resign from our company and he could not stand the silly customers.
correction: Mr. John said he wanted to resign from our company because he could not stand the silly customers.
explanation: The second clause gives the reason for the first, so use 'because' (cause/effect), not 'and' (which simply adds two separate ideas).
correct_examples: He quit because the work was too stressful.
She left early because she felt sick.
context: Writing a complaint sentence about an employee resigning

my_sentence: Dealing with a difficult customer is the stress for part of the job, so some people want to resign.
correction: Dealing with difficult customers is hard, so a lot of employees are resigning from our company.
explanation: 'Is the stress for part of the job' is not idiomatic. Use a simple adjective ('is hard' / 'is stressful'). Also prefer the general plural 'difficult customers'.
correct_examples: Dealing with difficult customers is hard.
Handling complaints all day is stressful.
context: Giving an opinion about a job using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: Playing basketball is very popular, so many children to join this kind of match.
correction: Playing basketball is very popular, so many children like to join the matches of this sport.
explanation: A clause needs a finite verb: 'many children' must be followed by a verb such as 'like' before the infinitive 'to join'. You cannot jump straight from the noun to 'to join'.
correct_examples: Many children like to join the matches.
A lot of students want to take part in the games.
context: Giving an opinion about playing basketball using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: Many children are like to join this match.
correction: Many children like to join the matches.
explanation: Don't add the auxiliary 'are' before a plain present-tense verb. With 'many children' (plural subject) use the base form directly: 'like', not 'are like'.
correct_examples: Many children like sports.
Most people enjoy weekends.
context: Giving an opinion about playing basketball using a gerund phrase

my_sentence: I can have enough time to prepare other things for other things, such as learning English or play with my daughter.
correction: I can have enough time to prepare for other things, such as learning English or playing with my daughter.
explanation: 'Prepare other things for other things' is redundant; say 'prepare for other things' once. Also keep the list parallel: 'learning' and 'playing' should both be gerunds. (assistant note)
correct_examples: I have time to prepare for other things, such as reading and exercising.
She spends evenings cooking and playing with her kids.
context: Explaining why he eats dinner early

my_sentence: Working from home can improve the efficiency, so more and more people apply from it.
correction: Working from home can improve efficiency, so more and more people apply for it.
explanation: The verb 'apply' takes the preposition 'for' when you mean to request something: 'apply for a job/it', not 'apply from it'.
correct_examples: She applied for the position last week.
Many people apply for remote work these days.
context: Giving an opinion about working from home using a gerund phrase
